cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of high-output gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 34.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 - (115 + 18) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old from a nursing home presents with volume depletion following ongoing lower gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 28.3 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 - (116 + 14) = 11, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 40-year-old presents with low GCS and hypoventilation after being found collapsed at home.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.5; measured 26.1 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old comes to ED with nausea and abdominal discomfort and recent weight loss. Associated features include dehydration and deep rapid breathing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~20.0 (+/-2); measured 21.1 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (103 + 8) = 23 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 61-year-old is brought to ED increased work of breathing with wheeze and a history of COPD.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old is brought to ED unwell with abdominal pain and recent weight loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 28.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (92 + 15) = 27 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old is brought to ED with increased drowsiness on a background of increased work of breathing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 26-year-old presents with acute dyspnoea, dizziness, and hand paraesthesiae with a normal cardiorespiratory examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.6; measured 20.6 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 36.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (113 + 18) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~50.5; measured 50.3 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old smoker presents with worsening dyspnoea, productive cough, and chronic exercise limitation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~31.6; measured 31.6 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (140 - (97 + 15) = 28) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.2; measured 27.3 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old presents after several days of infective symptoms, with hypercapnic symptoms and several days of breathlessness with wheeze.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.0; measured 31.3 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old is brought to ED with after missed insulin doses and with deep rapid breathing.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old is admitted with hypotensive and febrile.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old is brought to ED with with pneumonia and febrile.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 61-year-old is brought to ED worsening dyspnoea with increased sputum, worsening dyspnoea with increased sputum, and worsening dyspnoea with increased sputum.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.
